finding: Coze is investing heavily in workflow automation, matching where enterprise buyers want to consolidate tooling.
analysis: Workflow Templates lowers the activation bar for non-technical builders, expanding the addressable market beyond the existing developer base.
recommendation: Dify should prioritise a comparable template gallery and consider co-marketing with workflow-specific system integrators.

finding: Coze moved to a per-seat model that resembles the classic SaaS playbook and signals confidence in team expansion.
analysis: Per-seat pricing improves land-and-expand dynamics and is easier for procurement teams to evaluate than usage-based alternatives.
recommendation: Dify should publish a TCO comparison versus Coze for a 50-seat team to pre-empt pricing objections in active deals.

finding: Coze is hiring a VP of Engineering for its agent platform, indicating a step-change in platform investment.
analysis: VP-level hire typically precedes a major roadmap acceleration and signals ByteDance's continued commitment to Coze outside China.
recommendation: Watch the new VP's first 90 days of public appearances and roadmap announcements to anticipate where Coze will compete most aggressively.

finding: Coze launched native CRM connectors (Salesforce / HubSpot / Zoho), enabling direct integration into existing revenue stacks.
analysis: This collapses the time-to-value for sales and RevOps buyers and shortens Coze's evaluation cycle in mid-market accounts.
recommendation: Dify should ship at least one of these connectors or partner with a system integrator to provide equivalent coverage within the next quarter.

finding: ByteDance open-sourced the coze repository under Apache 2.0, accepting community contributions.
analysis: Open-sourcing is a community-building and developer-mindshare move, not necessarily a revenue move, but it pressures closed competitors on transparency.
recommendation: Audit the open-source release for enterprise-incompatible licensing terms (e.g. trademark / patent grants) and turn any gap into a Dify positioning point.

finding: FastGPT added multi-modal RAG, an area Dify has not yet addressed with first-class support.
analysis: Multi-modal retrieval is increasingly requested by enterprise buyers with image- and audio-heavy knowledge bases; the gap may show up in competitive deals.
recommendation: Open a Dify roadmap item for native image and audio ingestion in the next two quarters and pre-announce at the next community event.

finding: FastGPT 4.7 ships a visual DAG editor, narrowing the gap with Coze's workflow templates.
analysis: Visual DAG editors dramatically improve builder productivity; both Coze and FastGPT now have stronger no-code offerings than Dify.
recommendation: Prioritise the visual workflow editor in the Dify roadmap and benchmark it against both competitors before demoing to prospects.

finding: FastGPT has not yet published SOC2 documentation, which blocks deals with regulated buyers.
analysis: This is a short-term weakness but a near-term disqualifier for healthcare, financial services and EU public-sector prospects.
recommendation: Dify should highlight SOC2 Type II coverage in sales conversations with regulated buyers and offer to share the audit report under NDA.

finding: Users report degraded accuracy on FastGPT above ~60k context tokens.
analysis: Long-context accuracy regressions create churn risk in accounts with large knowledge bases and shift buyer attention to retrieval quality instead of model size.
recommendation: Dify should publish benchmarks on long-context accuracy and re-ranking to convert this weakness into a sales conversation.
